meta.description: Educational 'how people typically wheel this — and why / why-not' write-ups, one per name, grounded in the iv_data.json facts. Facts-first and descriptive; NOT advice.
meta.pairing: Join to iv_data.json on ticker. 'band' is the current-state grouping; 'writeup' is prose.
meta.disclaimer: Educational/informational only. NOT investment advice. May contain gaps or errors.

AA.characterization: **AA (Alcoa)** — large-cap ($14B) aluminum producer. IV ~53% (rank ~0.70), realized ~50%, +3-pt gap. **~44% below its 5yr peak**, not reclaimed, though it recovered an intermediate 3yr −52% drawdown (254d). Worst day −16.9% (April 2022). Liquid options at the near-term wheel tenors — ~9,144/day across all strikes over weeklies + the front monthly (≤~60 DTE).
AA.how_to: Given the implied volatility (~53%) and a deep, low-recovery drawdown (~44% below its peak), the premium is compensation for a low-recovery tail, not yield: a cash-secured put here is a commitment to own a deteriorated name, and if assigned the covered-call phase can stall (calls above the higher basis collect little; below locks in a loss). Defined-risk structures (a put spread) cap the downside.

AAL.characterization: **AAL (American Airlines)** — large-cap ($12B) airline. IV ~48% (rank ~0.55), realized ~49%, **−1.2-pt gap (negative VRP — premium under-compensates realized)**. **~21% below its 5yr peak (never reclaimed)**, though it recovered an intermediate 1yr −37% drawdown (86d) — a flicker of bounce capacity. Worst day −14.5% (April 2025). A high-debt airline in a moderate dip; lighter framing by depth (−21%), but note the negative VRP — the premium doesn't cover the realized swings here. Deeply liquid options at the near-term wheel tenors — ~51,728/day across all strikes over weeklies + the front monthly (≤~60 DTE).
AAL.how_to: Given the implied volatility (~48%) and a meaningful but not severe pullback (~21% below its peak), common practice runs the rotation with more caution: assignment lands moderately underwater, so covered-call income becomes the recovery play rather than a quick flip — sized as a name you'd be willing to hold, not a quick premium.

AAPL.characterization: **AAPL** — a mega-cap ($4.17T) consumer-tech company, and a frequent example of the "wheel a quality name you'd be content to own" approach. Measured implied vol ~24% (IV rank ~0.55, upper half of its range), realized ~22%, a thin ~1.3-point gap — single mega-caps are generally wheeled for the ownership and steadiness rather than a fat premium. Deeply liquid options at the near-term wheel tenors — ~240,543/day across all strikes over weeklies + the front monthly (≤~60 DTE).
AAPL.how_to: Given the moderate implied volatility (~24%) and shallow, historically-recoverable drawdowns (worst ~−33% over five years, since recovered), common practice is a low-maintenance rotation: lower-delta cash-secured puts — a smaller but steadier premium — taking assignment without alarm, then covered calls as routine income. A low-yield, low-maintenance rotation, not an income engine.

AMC.characterization: **AMC (AMC Entertainment)** — meme-era cinema chain. IV ~79% (rank ~0.71), realized ~54%, +24.8-pt gap (corporate-action-adjusted). **−99.5% over 5yr, −98.9% underwater.** Distinct mechanism — not leverage but **meme-collapse + massive dilution** (the 2022 APE issuance, 2023 reverse-split/conversion): share count exploded, price followed it toward zero. Doesn't come back — dilution mechanics, not a temporary dip. (worst day −36.8%, Sept 2023, adjusted.) Deeply liquid options at the near-term wheel tenors — ~98,674/day across all strikes over weeklies + the front monthly (≤~60 DTE).
AMC.how_to: Not a wheel candidate as a hold. The essentially −100% isn't an event to recover from — it's the instrument's mechanism: meme-collapse plus massive share dilution (the 2022 APE issuance, 2023 reverse-split), engineered to bleed toward zero. The premium is compensation for that structural decay, and assignment means owning a position designed to keep losing value — so most simply don't engage; at most defined-risk and short-dated, never a cash-secured put held for weeks.

On risk, from the facts: DIA's measured drawdowns are the shallowest of the four but still real, and have recovered. The worst 1-year decline was about **−10%** (recovered in ~55 days), the 3-year about **−17%** (~136 days), and the deepest 5-year episode about **−22%**, recovered in roughly **439 trading days**. The worst single day was about **−5.4%**. As with the other index baskets, the historical recoverability is the reason assignment is typically treated as a wait rather than a loss. Liquid options at the near-term wheel tenors — ~21,881/day across all strikes over weeklies + the front monthly (≤~60 DTE).
DIA.how_to: Given the low implied volatility (~13%) and market-level — not single-company — risk that has historically recovered (worst ~−22% over five years, since recovered), common practice is the canonical low-maintenance rotation: moderate-delta cash-secured puts, assignment taken without alarm given broad-index recoverability, then covered calls. A low-yield core rotation where the steadiness, not the income, is the point.
